ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311171); no specific entry for honey milk tea with less sugar and no topping. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no specific honey milk tea variant exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for honey milk tea beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of honey milk tea (approximately 350 ml / 350 g), as typically served at Singaporean bubble tea or specialty tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping), cr_id D08010055, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — milk tea and sweetened tea beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey milk tea (less sugar, no topping) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous sweetened milk tea entries in the Sydney GI Database and published literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Honey has a GI of approximately 55–58; dilution with tea and milk moderates the overall beverage GI. By the standard classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 34.23) / 100 ≈ 18.8, using a 350 g serving with 9.78 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Despite the low GI classification, the high glycaemic load per serving (18.8) reflects the large carbohydrate quantity delivered in a standard serving and remains clinically significant.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.78 g sugar per 100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; low sodium (13 mg/100 g) does not offset the cardiovascular risk from high sugar intake
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as independently raising blood pressure risk, and the 9.78 g/100 g sugar content exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold regardless of the low sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.78 g sugar per 100 g (all carbohydrate as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and a glycaemic load of ~18.8 per standard serving; liquid sugars from honey are rapidly absorbed and cause rapid postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the entirely sugar-derived carbohydrate load (9.78 g/100 g, 0 g fibre) in liquid form triggers rapid glycaemic response, and a 350 ml serving delivers approximately 34 g of sugar with a glycaemic load of ~18.8, well above safe limits for a single drink.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk; fat content (2.41 g/100 g) may include saturated fat from non-dairy creamer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage; the 9.78 g/100 g sugar content and potential saturated fat from non-dairy creamer contribute to dyslipidaemia risk, and substitution with unsweetened plain tea is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (9.78 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3s, polyphenols, antioxidants); non-dairy creamer may contain pro-inflammatory palm oil
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the high added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation relevant to RA flares, and the absence of any anti-inflammatory components provides no offsetting benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Milk Tea (Less Sugar, No Topping) is a freshly prepared Singaporean specialty tea beverage made by combining brewed black tea with milk and a reduced quantity of honey, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. The drink is a lighter, lower-sugar variant of the popular honey milk tea sold at bubble tea and specialty tea stalls across Singapore. Its flavour profile is mildly sweet with a subtle floral honey note, a creamy mouthfeel from the milk component, and the characteristic astringency of black tea. Per 100 g, it provides 60 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 9.78 g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 13 mg sodium. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, the beverage still contains 9.78 g sugar per 100 g — equivalent to approximately 34 g of sugar in a standard 350 ml serving — classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both HPB Singapore and AHA definitions. This SSB classification makes it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The liquid form of the honey-derived sugars (fructose and glucose) accelerates absorption and postprandial glycaemic response compared to solid foods. The fat content likely derives from non-dairy creamer, which may contain palm oil and saturated fat. Clinically, this beverage should be treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a daily drink, and unsweetened plain tea is the preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact food item (cr_id D08010055) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific beverage variant.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this freshly prepared honey milk tea variant are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D08010055) as of 2026. 2. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-containing solid components. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea and honey GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this specific beverage exists in approved sources. By the standard WHO/University of Sydney classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 is categorised as low. Despite the low GI, the glycaemic load per standard 350 g serving is ~18.8, which is clinically significant. 4. The 'less sugar' designation reduces but does not eliminate the sugar-sweetened beverage classification; 9.78 g sugar/100 g still exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold. 5. Fat content (2.41 g/100 g) likely derives from non-dairy creamer; saturated fat content should be verified from product label. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311171 (milk tea) and FoodEx2 A0FKP (tea-based beverages with milk) used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for honey milk tea less sugar no topping.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, populated all mandatory sections, applied Singaporean multilingual naming rules.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent'. Reviewed gi_category assignment.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55 per the standard WHO/University of Sydney GI classification boundary (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source source_match_notes and notes field to document the low GI classification and the clinical significance of the high glycaemic load per serving despite the low GI. This resolves the q4_glycemic_completeness failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
